stem: An extraction schema marks `attendee_count` as required, but many source articles never state attendance. What does this schema design cause?
options: Validation errors on every article without a count
The extractor skips those articles entirely
Structural pressure to fabricate — the model must produce some value, so it invents plausible ones
Slower extraction while the model searches for the value
rationales: The output will validate fine — that's the problem. The invented number is schema-legal.
Extraction still runs; the schema just forces a value into the field.
A required field the source may not contain converts honest absence into invention. Make it optional or nullable so the model can say `null` truthfully.
Latency isn't the failure mode; fabricated data is.

stem: An equipment classifier uses a strict enum (`laptop`, `monitor`, `printer`), but new device categories keep appearing and validation failures pile up. What is the standard fix?
options: Add an `other` enum value paired with a detail field capturing the source's actual wording
Remove the enum and accept free-text categories
Retrain reviewers to map new devices onto the closest existing category
Regenerate the schema weekly from observed categories
rationales: The `other` + `*_detail` pattern handles long-tail categories without schema rewrites: stable categories stay machine-usable, novel ones preserve source wording for later normalization.
Free text loses the machine-usability the enum existed to provide.
Forcing wrong categories corrupts data to satisfy the schema — the inverse of fixing it.
Constant schema churn breaks every consumer downstream; the escape hatch avoids the churn entirely.

stem: Reviewers find fabricated values in extractions, and the team proposes a second LLM call to verify each extraction against the source. Why is this generally inferior to fixing the schema?
options: Verification calls only work on documents under 10 pages
Two calls double the rate limit consumption, which providers prohibit
Verification models use different tokenizers, causing mismatches

It adds cost and latency, can rationalize the original answer, and leaves the root cause — a schema demanding values the source lacks — untouched; allowing `null` lets the first call signal absence directly
rationales: Document length isn't the issue.
Nothing prohibits it — it's just an expensive non-fix.
Tokenizers are irrelevant here.

The fabrication is caused by the schema's demand. A verifier can hallucinate too, and agrees with plausible inventions. Fix the incentive; keep verification passes as sampling-based audits on already-good pipelines.

stem: For a review-summarization schema, when should `pros` be `null` rather than an empty array?
options: Never — empty arrays and null are interchangeable
When the document didn't address pros at all — an empty array claims "the reviewer mentioned no pros," which is a different assertion
When the array would exceed five items
When the review is positive overall
rationales: They encode different claims, and downstream consumers will treat them differently.
Absence semantics matter: empty array = "explicitly none," null = "not addressed." Choosing deliberately keeps extractions honest for short or partial sources.
Size has nothing to do with the null/empty distinction.
Overall sentiment doesn't change what the document did or didn't address.

stem: Contracts contain amendments, and different sections state conflicting notice periods. Reviewers must audit every extracted value. What does the schema need?
options: Higher-confidence extraction with `temperature: 0`
A post-processing step that picks the most common value
Provenance fields — `source_location`, `source_quote`, `effective_date` — and separate capture of original vs amended values rather than one scalar
A second extraction pass that only reads amendments
rationales: Temperature affects sampling variability, not conflicting sources or auditability.
Frequency isn't precedence — an amendment stated once beats an original value stated three times.
When documents amend and conflict, a single scalar is the wrong shape. Provenance fields make every value traceable and let reviewers audit the model's choice against the exact source text.
A separate amendments pass still needs provenance to justify which value won, and fragments the extraction.

stem: A team wants API-level citations attached to every field of a strict JSON structured output. What is the architectural reality?
options: Citations and strict JSON structured outputs may be incompatible — citations need interleaved citation blocks while schemas constrain the JSON — so represent source locations explicitly as fields in your schema
Citations attach automatically to any JSON field when enabled
Citations work with JSON if you lower the schema's strictness setting
Citations replace the need for provenance fields in all extraction designs
rationales: The two features have conflicting output requirements. For structured extraction with provenance, the schema itself should carry `source_location`-style fields.
There is no automatic per-field citation attachment for constrained JSON.
Strictness settings don't reconcile interleaved citation blocks with constrained JSON output.
Citations suit narrative answers over documents; structured extraction still needs explicit provenance fields.

stem: Invoice extractions keep showing line items that don't sum to the stated total. What schema addition catches this without asking the model to reconcile values it can't verify?
options: A `verified: true` field the model sets after checking its own math
Explicit reconciliation fields — `calculated_total`, `stated_total`, `totals_match` — so mismatches are flagged mechanically
A required comment field explaining any discrepancy
Rejecting any extraction where totals differ
rationales: Self-certification is the thing being doubted; the model asserting "verified" adds no evidence.
Reconciliation fields expose both numbers so application code flags mismatches automatically — catching OCR errors and extraction mistakes alike, with no self-reconciliation demanded of the model.
Free-text explanations aren't machine-checkable and invite rationalization.
Sometimes the mismatch is real (the invoice itself is wrong) — that's information to surface, not an extraction failure to reject.

stem: In which failure case are retries with feedback UNPRODUCTIVE, no matter how good the correction prompt is?
options: A locale-formatted number ("1,234") needs to become the integer 1234
The source provides a nested object but the schema wants a flat array
An ISO datetime needs truncating to a date
The required information lives in an external document that was never provided to the model
rationales: Trivially fixed on retry with feedback.
Restructuring is exactly what feedback-guided retries fix well.
A formatting fix the model handles immediately once told.
No retry can extract what isn't in the context — further attempts only produce hallucinated values. Retrieve the missing source or route to human review.

stem: A meeting transcript fits in context, but decisions and dollar amounts are scattered across hours of unrelated chatter, and extractions keep missing details. What is the highest-leverage change?
options: Add a pre-extraction pass that surfaces relevant sections — decisions, action items, amounts, dates — into a structured intermediate, then extract from that
Add more few-shot examples of complete extractions
Chunk the transcript and extract from each chunk independently
Switch to a model with a larger context window
rationales: For long-but-in-context sources with buried facts, a model-driven pre-extraction pass keeps attention on what matters and substantially cuts missed or conflated details — better than chunking or more examples.
Few-shot helps unusual extraction patterns; it doesn't help find needles in a haystack.
Chunking spreads the haystack across requests and loses cross-chunk relationships (a decision revised an hour later).
The document already fits — capacity isn't the constraint, attention is.

stem: A pipeline is 97% accurate overall, and leadership wants to auto-approve high-confidence extractions. What must happen first?
options: Lower the threshold gradually while monitoring complaints
Nothing — 97% exceeds the human baseline

Break accuracy down by document type, field, and confidence band against a labeled set — aggregates can hide a segment running at 80% — then keep stratified sampling of auto-approved outputs after launch
A/B test several thresholds and keep the best F1
rationales: Complaints are lagging, incomplete feedback — systematic errors that look plausible never get reported.
Beating humans on average says nothing about where the pipeline fails.
Segment-level analysis is the prerequisite; uncalibrated confidence can't define a safe threshold, and post-launch stratified sampling catches hidden error patterns.
Threshold optimization before segmentation tunes against a misleading aggregate.
sourceRef: ## 4 > Confidence and Human Review (Validating Automation Plans)

stem: Documents arrive with mixed urgency: most tolerate delay, some have tight SLAs. How should batch vs real-time processing be assigned?
options: Batch everything and mark urgent documents as high priority within the batch
Route per-document: standard documents to the Batch API for cost savings, urgent ones to the real-time Messages API
Process everything real-time to be safe
Alternate batches hourly between urgent and standard queues
rationales: Batches can't expedite items inside them — batch latency is precisely why urgent documents don't belong there.
Routing is per-document, not per-workload: each document goes to the API whose latency profile matches its deadline. Join batch results by `custom_id`, since order isn't guaranteed.
Paying real-time prices for delay-tolerant volume forfeits the batch discount for nothing.
Urgent items still wait for a batch window; the SLA problem remains.

stem: Corrections data shows informal measurements ("a handful," "a splash") get invented or omitted in 23% of cases. What is the highest-leverage fix?
options: Fine-tune a custom model on the corrections dataset
Add a regex post-processor that detects informal units
Create a new schema field for measurement style
Add a few-shot example demonstrating the correct handling — extracting the informal phrase verbatim
rationales: Fine-tuning is a heavyweight intervention to try only if focused prompt improvements fail to move the metric.
Regex can find informal units but can't teach the model what to do with them.
A new field doesn't change the invent-or-omit behavior.
For a clear recurring failure mode, one demonstrating example is usually the cheapest, most effective fix — examples teach subtle handling better than narrative rules.
